summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the common open source response of 'code it yourself' as unrealistic for most users and even many programmers. He argues that mastering an unfamiliar codebase just to implement a small feature requires time most people don't have. Instead, he proposes financial sponsorship as a middle ground between giving up and DIY coding. He shares his own experience sponsoring bugfixes for Stack Overflow's dependencies and wonders why aggregated sponsorship platforms haven't naturally emerged in the open source ecosystem.
key_points: The 'code it yourself' response to feature requests often functions as a polite dismissal rather than a genuine solution
Even skilled programmers rarely have time to master alien codebases for small improvements
Financial sponsorship offers a practical middle path for non-coders to influence project direction
Pooled funding could attract developer attention when individual requests wouldn't
The absence of natural evolution toward aggregated sponsorship systems raises questions about sustainability
money_quotes: Sometimes, when people say 'why don't you code it yourself?' they're really saying 'F**k you.'
Surely there's something between the extremes of 'give up' and 'code it yourself.'
Sponsorship seems like a constructive way for people who are unable or unwilling to write code to affect the direction of a project.

Even if you're the type of macho, gung-ho programmer who can master an alien code base just to get some small pet feature or bugfix in – do you honestly have the kind of time it would take to do that?
I am concerned that this sort of aggregated sponsorship system hasn't naturally evolved on its own by now.
I was happy to give back to the project, and it was certainly a more realistic option than us carving out a chunk of our own development time to contribute the bugfixes ourselves.
key_insight: Financial sponsorship bridges the gap between passive users and active contributors in open source, offering a realistic alternative to the false choice between coding it yourself and accepting the status quo.
